Title: Hello, Blob!
Standard: Create and display a Blob using setup()/draw() and RGB color.
GettingStarted: Not running yet, or no blob appears. Next: check the import line.
GotItWorking: Sketch runs and a Blob appears on screen.
MadeItMine: Deliberately changed color, size, position, or background — and can say why.
WentBeyond: Took the blob somewhere the lesson didn't ask for — a second blob, a small scene, something of their own.

Title: Draw Your Character
Standard: Create a custom extends Blob class and override drawSprite() to draw a character anchored at pos.x/pos.y.
GettingStarted: Tab won't compile or nothing draws. Next: check the class line and that super(...) is copied exactly.
GotItWorking: A character of their own appears, built from a few shapes anchored to pos.x/pos.y.
MadeItMine: Character is recognizably their own — added/changed shapes, colors, and details.
WentBeyond: Pushed the art somewhere new — layered shapes, a non-circular body, a second character class, an idea of their own.

Title: Bring It to Life
Standard: Use setVelocity() and move() to animate the character, with screen wrapping and random() for variety.
GettingStarted: Character is frozen. Next: make sure player.move() is called inside draw().
GotItWorking: Character moves on its own and wraps around the screen edges.
MadeItMine: Chose a velocity with its own feel (fast/slow/diagonal/random) and a matching background mood.
WentBeyond: Explored motion further — random() velocities, multiple moving sprites, a behavior of their own.

Title: You're in Control
Standard: Control the character with followMouse() or keyboardControl(), distinguishing continuous from event input.
GettingStarted: No response to input. Next: followMouse needs setVelocity() > 0; keyboardControl() must be in keyPressed(), not draw().
GotItWorking: Can drive the character with the mouse or the arrow keys.
MadeItMine: Picked and tuned the control style that fits the game (a speed that feels right, or a fun mousePressed()).
WentBeyond: Combined or invented controls — keyboard plus a click ability, a second self-moving sprite, a scheme of their own.

Title: A World of Objects
Standard: Store many objects in an ArrayList and process them with counting and for-each loops; use random() for variety.
GettingStarted: List of objects isn't appearing. Next: counting loop creates, for-each loop draws; call drawSprite() on each item, not the list.
GotItWorking: Many objects appear and move — created in a counting loop, drawn in a for-each loop.
MadeItMine: Tuned the world — changed count/size/speed, added a second list, or spawns objects on click.
WentBeyond: Built their own world piece — a custom environment sprite class, a system, an idea the lesson didn't ask for.

Title: If This, Then That
Standard: Use if statements, changing variables (int score), and text() to make the program react and track state.
GettingStarted: Score/text won't show or the if never triggers. Next: declare variables at the top, outside setup/draw.
GotItWorking: A variable changes during play, shows with text(), and an if branches the game.
MadeItMine: Changed what triggers the state change, restyled the game-over screen, or added a variable like lives.
WentBeyond: Made the game react in layered ways of their own — combined conditions/variables, a responsive system they designed.

Title: Collisions!
Standard: Use collidesWith() with if to trigger game events, remove from a list safely, and connect collisions to score and state.
GettingStarted: Collisions not registering, or collecting crashes the sketch. Next: use the backwards loop when removing items.
GotItWorking: Player collects coins on contact, score rises, coins disappear without errors.
MadeItMine: Added their own twist — hazards to dodge, spawning, or a changed win condition.
WentBeyond: Built a collision idea of their own — a dodge mechanic, splitting objects, a consequence they invented.

Title: Build Your Game World
Standard: Use Block for terrain, control draw order for layering, and combine static and moving sprites to design a world.
GettingStarted: Layering is wrong or the ground hides the player. Next: later draws sit on top; Block x,y is the top-left corner.
GotItWorking: Scene has a background, Block terrain, and a decoration, layered sensibly.
MadeItMine: Designed a themed world that reads as a place, not placeholders.
WentBeyond: Built the world somewhere the lesson didn't ask for — a custom decoration class, layering/transparency, terrain that shapes play.

Title: Build Your Game (Capstone)
Standard: Combine all prior skills into a complete, playable, replayable mini-game with win/lose conditions and independent design decisions.
GettingStarted: Not yet playable. Next: get one full loop working first; reset via restartGame().
GotItWorking: Playable: collectibles, a win and/or lose condition, a score, a working restart.
MadeItMine: Unmistakably their game — themed world, own rules and difficulty, deliberate design decisions.
WentBeyond: Pushed past the assignment in a direction they chose — a mechanic that deepens play, a rule nobody handed them, a design call that's theirs (not cosmetics).

Title: Bonus: Images
Standard: Add images via the data/ folder, display decorations with ImageSprite, and use a PImage inside an extends Blob character so collisions work.
GettingStarted: Image draws as a blank/colored box or not at all. Next: file must be in data/ with an exact name; box usually means no PNG transparency.
GotItWorking: Their own image appears — as an ImageSprite decoration and/or as their character.
MadeItMine: Themed the look with their own art — a character image plus decorations (or a background image), sized to fit.
WentBeyond: Took images further — a picture-character that still collides, drifting art, swapping images to fake animation, an idea of their own.

Title: Bonus: Sounds
Standard: Install the Sound library, load a SoundFile from data/, play one-shot effects on events, and loop background music.
GettingStarted: No sound or a null-pointer error. Next: install the Sound library and put files in data/ with exact names.
GotItWorking: A sound effect plays on a game event, triggered from the right spot in the code.
MadeItMine: Chose sounds that fit the game and wired them to their own events — collect, hazard, or looping music.
WentBeyond: Layered audio thoughtfully — music under effects with balanced volume, milestone sounds, guarded playback, a touch of their own.
